content:
The Cache Staleness Problem

Kubernetes controllers look simple from the outside: watch for changes, reconcile desired state with actual state, repeat. The reality is more subtle. controller-runtime serves reads from an in-memory informer cache backed by a watch stream from the API server. This cache is eventually consistent — after you write an object, the cache may take several seconds to reflect the new state.

If your reconciler reads from the cache immediately after writing, it can see the pre-write version of the object and make decisions based on stale data. The failure mode: your reconciler runs, sees the object in state A, updates it to state B, then immediately reads from cache and sees state A again, decides the update didn't take, and writes state B again. Two writes instead of one. Usually harmless — unless your creation logic is not idempotent, in which case you get duplicate child resources.

The fix

Never read from the informer cache immediately after a write. Re-fetch from the API server using client.Get with the object's NamespacedName. Treat cache reads as advisory — good enough for the initial reconcile trigger, not trustworthy for post-mutation reads. This adds one API server round-trip per reconcile but eliminates the entire class of stale-cache-driven duplicate writes.

Optimistic Locking and the 409

Kubernetes uses resourceVersion as an optimistic lock. When you submit an update, the API server checks that your resourceVersion matches the current version in etcd. If it doesn't — because another writer updated the object between your read and your write — you get a 409 Conflict. controller-runtime re-queues on 409 automatically.

The problem: if your reconciler reads from the informer cache (which may be stale), constructs an update based on that stale state, and submits it with a resourceVersion that's already been superseded, you'll 409, re-queue, read from cache again (still stale), and loop until the cache catches up. In a high-throughput cluster, this loop can dominate reconcile latency.

patch.MergeFrom instead of Update

The structural fix is using patch.MergeFrom instead of client.Update. A merge patch sends only the fields you changed — the API server merges them into the current object state regardless of resourceVersion. This eliminates 409s on spec updates entirely. For status updates, use the status subresource (client.Status().Update) so spec and status updates don't conflict with each other when both paths are active.

Finalizers and the Deletion Race

Finalizers are the correct mechanism for ensuring cleanup happens before an object is deleted. Register a finalizer on creation, check for deletionTimestamp on every reconcile, run cleanup if set, remove the finalizer when done. Kubernetes will not delete the object until all finalizers are cleared.

The race condition: your controller crashes between running cleanup and removing the finalizer. On restart, it sees the deletionTimestamp and runs cleanup again. If your cleanup is not idempotent — if running it twice deletes something that should not be deleted twice — you have a production incident waiting to happen. The discipline: every cleanup operation must be idempotent. Check before deleting. Use server-side apply. Treat "resource already gone" as success, not error.

Level-Triggered vs Edge-Triggered

The most important design decision in a Kubernetes controller is level-triggered reconciliation. Edge-triggered means reacting to events: "this object was updated, reconcile it." Level-triggered means reconciling the full desired state on every trigger regardless of what changed. The difference matters when your controller misses events — which it will, during restarts, leader election transitions, or API server connectivity blips.

An edge-triggered controller that misses a "workload deleted" event will leave orphaned resources indefinitely. A level-triggered controller that misses events will catch up on the next reconcile trigger. Level-triggered is harder to write correctly — every reconcile must converge to the desired state from any arbitrary starting state — but it is the only design that is correct under real cluster conditions.

The practical test

The correctness test for a level-triggered controller: kill the controller pod mid-reconcile (with kubectl delete pod) and verify that the cluster converges to the correct state after restart without manual intervention. Run this test at 500+ CRs, not just at small scale. The quadratic placement algorithm bug I found only manifested above 200 CRs — it was invisible in unit tests.

Status Conditions Are a Contract

Status conditions are not logging — they are a machine-readable contract between your controller and the rest of the platform. Following the type/status/reason/message convention from the Kubernetes API conventions means kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready works out of the box, Argo CD can read health status without a custom health check, and every Kubernetes observability tool can surface your controller's state without custom instrumentation.

01

The most important property of a controller is not correctness on the happy path — it is correctness after an arbitrary crash at any point in the reconcile loop. Design for crash-safety from the start. Retrofitting it is much harder.

02

Leader election default parameters (15s lease duration, 10s renew deadline) mean a failover takes up to 15 seconds. For latency-sensitive placement decisions, tuning to 5s/4s reduced observed failover time from under 15s to under 5s with acceptable etcd write amplification.

03

Watch cache staleness is not a bug — it is a documented property of the informer architecture. The correct mental model: the cache is an optimization for read throughput, not a source of truth. Always re-fetch before any mutation that depends on current state.

04

The MaxConcurrentReconciles setting in controller-runtime defaults to 1. For workloads where reconciles are mostly I/O bound (API server calls, external system checks), increasing this to 4–8 dramatically improves throughput. For CPU-bound reconciles, it adds contention. Profile first.
